description: A person working as a 'kima' writes or copies records in durable form. A scribe may record another person's words, reproduce an existing document, or maintain an institutional record. 'thekiro' (write) is the broader act.
articulatory_notes: Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release into stressed high-front unrounded 'i'. Close the lips for voiced nasal 'm' and finish with central 'a'.
sound_symbolism: A clean 'k' closure at the back of the mouth opens into narrow 'i', followed by the softer closure of the lips at 'm'. Those two brief contacts can feel like deliberate marks placed one after another.
usage_notes: 'kima' is the role. When the scribe records someone else's wording, the source of the words and the scribe's own contribution remain distinct. Copying, editing, translating, and composing are separate acts unless the account includes more than one.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Clear attribution keeps the recorder's authority from being mistaken for the source's own voice. It also makes the scribe's labour visible.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Hand copying preserved laws, accounts, stories, and correspondence, with accuracy depending on careful reading and repeated checking.

semantic_domains.communication: writing or copying another person's words and public records
semantic_domains.community: a recognised role in keeping durable records
semantic_domains.creation: the skilled production and reproduction of written documents
